requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":36,"created_at":"2021-06-12T13:47:18Z","updated_at":"2024-07-17T21:09:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Original Title: **Change `fill([0, 0], 2)` behavior for 2.0**

The behavior of `fill` when its first argument is an array is to create an array of arrays, where each of the elements is a reference to the exact same array in memory. This is counterintuitive to many users (see Discourse links below), and it seems kind of pointless. I propose that in 2.0 `fill` with an array input should fill the resulting array with *copies* of the input array.

Below are some examples where this came up on Discourse. There may be more.

https://discourse.julialang.org/t/simple-question-about-assignment-to-a-vector-of-vectors/62744
https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fill-anarray-2-behaviour/22429
https://discourse.julialang.org/t/initialization-of-array-of-arrays-with-fill-ones-1-2-2-only-one-vector-is-created/48048
https://discourse.julialang.org/t/how-can-i-fill-an-array-with-empty-2d-arrays/60895","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209/reactions","total_count":17,"+1":4,"-1":13,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2234301337","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209#issuecomment-2234301337","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/41209","id":2234301337,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6FLLuZ","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-17T21:09:25Z","updated_at":"2024-07-17T21:09:25Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I wonder if VSCode/Lint could help flag places where any value in a `fill` is mutated? Given the following source file `foo.jl`:
```julia
foo(a, c = false) = 1
foo(a, b, c = false) = 2
```

Run the following in the REPL:
```julia
julia> include("../src/foo.jl")
foo (generic function with 3 methods)

julia> foo(1)
2

julia> @edit foo(1)
```

Notice that `foo(1)` calls the three-parameter method; however, `@edit foo(1)` takes you to the two-parameter method. I would expect `@edit` to behave the same way as the real dispatch.

Side note that `foo(1)` calling the three-parameter method is confusing, but probably can't be changed before Julia 2.0...

```

```julia
# foo(a, c = false) = 1
foo(a) = foo(a, false) # method 1a
foo(a, c) = 1          # method 1b
# foo(a, b, c = false) = 2
foo(a, b) = foo(a, b, false) # method 2a (overwrites method 1b)
foo(a, b, c) = 2             # method 2b
```

This is confusing, but `@edit` isn't wrong — it's calling methods `1a`  -> `2a` -> `2b`.  The entry point is indeed because you defined the first pair of methods.

We do sometimes warn when overwriting methods, but I've lost the thread on what the conditions are that make that happen.  Perhaps this should be a similar warning?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2245350086/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

is a very simple change that will hopefully help folks identify things like https://discourse.julialang.org/t/inconsistent-cpu-utilisation-in-threads-loops/110512 a little faster.

Before:

```julia
julia> @time @sync begin
           Threads.@spawn @ccall sleep(1::Cuint)::Cuint
           sleep(.5)
           Threads.@spawn GC.gc()
       end
  1.078393 seconds (927 allocations: 65.078 KiB, 5.85% gc time, 1.19% compilation time)
```

After:

```julia
julia> @time @sync begin
           Threads.@spawn @ccall sleep(1::Cuint)::Cuint
           sleep(.5)
           Threads.@spawn GC.gc()
       end

here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` have one argument that is either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously `init` was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` have one argument that is either `init`
or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":4,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2248278518","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945#issuecomment-2248278518","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945","id":2248278518,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6GAgH2","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-24T15:15:50Z","updated_at":"2024-07-24T15:15:50Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I don't like requiring that `init` needs to be a left-argument to `op` because that means that `foldr` would not be a valid implementation for `reduce`. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55238","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55238/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55238/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55238/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55238","id":2428462281,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6Qv2TJ","number":55238,"title":"Clarify map/mapreduce's documentation with respect to memoization","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-24T21:00:19Z","updated_at":"2024-07-24T21:00:20Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is split out from https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945#discussion_r1571584741:

Both `map` and `mapreduce` sometimes re-use return values — most notably with sparse data structures.  `map`'s docs don't mention this, whereas `mapreduce` explicitly allows for it — but neither documentation is ideal.  It'd be nice to more finely scope this behavior along the lines of @MasonProtter's comment:

> I think it's a sensible corner case to make it clear to people that we're not normally in the buisness of memoizing reductions, but if a datastructure has its own memoization scheme then we'll reserve the right to also take advantage of that.

There are probably more functions like these — surely including broadcast — that'd be good to evaluate at the same time.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55238/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55238/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1690424559","pull_request_review_id":2197759929,"id":1690424559,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5kwdTv","diff_hunk":"@@ -280,29 +280,65 @@ mapreduce_impl(f, op, A::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted, ifirst::Integer, ilast::Int
 """
     mapreduce(f, op, itrs...; [init])
 
-Apply function `f` to each element(s) in `itrs`, and then reduce the result using the binary
-function `op`. If provided, `init` must be a neutral element for `op` that will be returned
-for empty collections. It is unspecified whether `init` is used for non-empty collections.
-In general, it will be necessary to provide `init` to work with empty collections.
+Apply function `f` to each element(s) in `itrs`, and then repeatedly call the 2 argument
+function `op` with those results or results from previous `op` evaluations until a single value is returned.
+
+If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
+an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
+Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` have one argument that is either `init`
+or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
+unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that
+does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
+with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
+identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.
+
+In contrast with [`mapfoldl`](@ref) and [`mapfoldr`](@ref), the sequence of
+function evaluations and the associativity of the reduction is not specified
+and may vary between different methods and across Julia versions. Some implementations
+may reuse the return value of `f` for elements that appear multiple times in the
+collection(s).","path":"base/reduce.jl","commit_id":"f48a2c21ee576a560f7a2608f55e713a3187ca50","original_commit_id":"05c9e7a668446b95430d212bcc1e1abeef56a01c","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I'd ask that this be tackled separately — I fiddled around with talking about memoized/sparse/something, but it's so hard to talk about in the abstract. here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` have one argument that is either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously `init` was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` have one argument that is either `init`
or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` have one argument that is either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously `init` was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` have one argument that is either `init`
or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes is split out from https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945#discussion_r1571584741:

Both `map` and `mapreduce` sometimes re-use return values — most notably with sparse data structures.  `map`'s docs don't mention this, whereas `mapreduce` explicitly allows for it — but neither documentation is ideal.  It'd be nice to more finely scope this behavior along the lines of @MasonProtter's comment:

> I think it's a sensible corner case to make it clear to people that we're not normally in the buisness of memoizing reductions, but if a datastructure has its own memoization scheme then we'll reserve the right to also take advantage of that.

There are probably more functions like these — including broadcast — that'd be good to evaluate at the same time.

```Julia
julia> using SparseArrays

julia> map(x->x+rand(), spzeros(3))
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 3 stored entries:
  [1]  =  0.538611
  [2]  =  0.538611
  [3]  =  0.538611

julia> broadcast(x->x+rand(), spzeros(3))
3-element SparseVector{Float64, Int64} with 3 stored entries:
  [1]  =  0.983621
  [2]  =  0.983621
  [3]  =  0.983621

etc."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":14,"created_at":"2024-04-03T22:10:38Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T16:54:08Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53945","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` have one argument that is either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously `init` was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` have one argument that is either `init`
or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":4,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2250976217","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945#issuecomment-2250976217","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945","id":2250976217,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6GKyvZ","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-25T16:54:07Z","updated_at":"2024-07-25T16:54:07Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I have two reasons why that's not a major concern in my view.  One is that there are three "types" of arguments that get passed to `op`:
* `init` itself
* values from `itr` — which necessarily could be either left- or right-args (but not both when `init` is provided)
* return values from previous `op` calls — which are also necessarily left- or right-args (or both)

Because the `init` value is also the return value for empty collections, it should be of a similar "type" (yes, I mean that both as a literal `Type` and as a softer "interpretation" or "category of thing") as `op`'s return values.  And `op` necessarily needs to be able to accommodate previous return values in either slot.

The second is more simplistic, but it really seems like `foldr` should be a valid implementation for `reduce`, and `foldr(=>, [1,2,3], init=0)` is (and should be) `1 => (2 => (3 => 0))`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2250976217/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

With `init`, it will pass *all* its `itr` values as right-args.  All the left args (except one) are results from previous operations.  And `init` is that final left-arg.  Flip the associativity with `-r` and the argument orderings flip.  So `init`'s placement flips.  It makes sense that an arbitrary associativity would grant this leeway.

If care about argument orderings between `init` and an `itr` value, you probably care about the argument orderings between an `op` result and an `itr` value — and so then you care about the associativity.  And you should use `foldl` or `foldr`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2251018524/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1693205501","pull_request_review_id":2202056651,"id":1693205501,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5k7EP9","diff_hunk":"@@ -93,6 +93,88 @@ function _dropdims(A::AbstractArray, dims::Dims)
 end
 _dropdims(A::AbstractArray, dim::Integer) = _dropdims(A, (Int(dim),))
 
+
+"""
+    insertdims(A; dims)
+
+Return an array with the same data as `A`, but with singleton dimensions specified by
+`dims` inserted.
+The dimensions of `A` and `dims` must be contiguous.
+If dimensions occur multiple times in `dims`, several singleton dimensions are inserted.
+
+The result shares the same underlying data as `A`, such that the
+result is mutable if and only if `A` is mutable, and setting elements of one
+alters the values of the other.
+
+See also: [`reshape`](@ref), [`dropdims`](@ref), [`vec`](@ref).
+
+# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
+2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
+ 1  2
+ 3  4
+
+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
+1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =
+ 1
+
+[:, :, 2, 1] =
+ 3
+
+[:, :, 1, 2] =
+ 2
+
+[:, :, 2, 2] =
+ 4
+
+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
+1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =
+ 1  3
+
+[:, :, 1, 2] =
+ 2  4
+
+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
+1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =
+ 1  3
+
+[:, :, 2, 1] =
+ 2  4
+
+julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
+2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
+ 5  2
+ 3  4
+```
+
+!!! compat "Julia 1.12"
+    Requires Julia 1.12 or later.
+"""
+insertdims(A; dims) = _insertdims(A, dims)
+function _insertdims(A::AbstractArray{T, N}, dims::Tuple{Vararg{Int64, M}}) where {T, N, M}
+    maximum(dims) ≤ ndims(A)+1 || throw(ArgumentError("The largest entry in dims must be ≤ ndims(A) + 1."))
+    1 ≤ minimum(dims) || throw(ArgumentError("The smallest entry in dims must be ≥ 1."))
+    issorted(dims) || throw(ArgumentError("dims=$(dims) are not sorted"))
+
+    # n is the amount of the dims already inserted
+    ax_n = Base._foldoneto(((ds, n, dims), _) ->
+                             dims != Tuple(()) && n == first(dims) ?
+                             ((ds..., Base.OneTo(1)), n, Base.tail(dims)) :
+                             ((ds..., axes(A,n)), n+1, dims),
+                           ((), 1, dims), Val(ndims(A) + length(dims)))
+           # we need only the new shape and not n
+           reshape(A, ax_n[1])
+","path":"base/abstractarraymath.jl","commit_id":"8648d84f5a910344acc8573828924fb843b95639","original_commit_id":"8648d84f5a910344acc8573828924fb843b95639","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Looks like this is duplicated? all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4

all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4
```","created_at":"2022-06-23T15:08:52Z","updated_at":"2024-07-26T14:45:50Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"653e42a7fa30901909b92dcfbd8eb83f37a5a2c8","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:arrays","name":"domain:arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:

all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4
```","created_at":"2022-06-23T15:08:52Z","updated_at":"2024-07-26T15:27:43Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"e936f336219f941f2ac4fa1db12b299eaf7b6a8d","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:arrays","name":"domain:arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":25,"created_at":"2022-06-23T15:08:52Z","updated_at":"2024-07-26T15:35:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/45793","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45793","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45793.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45793.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Hi all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45793/reactions","total_count":2,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":2,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45793/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2253015004","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/45793#issuecomment-2253015004","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45793","id":2253015004,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6GSkfc","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-26T15:35:45Z","updated_at":"2024-07-26T15:35:45Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think this is a very good addition.  My only slight hesitation is with the specific method signatures, specifically that `insertdims(A::Any; dims)` supports _all_ types and defers to the internal `_insertdims`, which will throw internal method errors for everything except `(A::AbstractArray, dims::Union{Integer, Tuple{Vararg{Int64}}})`.  But this is completely and totally allayed by the fact that this is _precisely_ how `dropdims` works.  It makes _so much sense_ for these two functions to have symmetrical definitions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2253015004/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

 _dropdims(A::AbstractArray, dim::Integer) = _dropdims(A, (Int(dim),))
 
+
+"""
+    insertdims(A; dims)
+
+Return an array with the same data as `A`, but with singleton dimensions specified by
+`dims` inserted.
+The dimensions of `A` and `dims` must be contiguous.","path":"base/abstractarraymath.jl","commit_id":"080c83c985249161d197de68af33b3ab96ba8402","original_commit_id":"080c83c985249161d197de68af33b3ab96ba8402","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I do think this could be worded slightly better and could help address the other point simultaneously. all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4
```","created_at":"2022-06-23T15:08:52Z","updated_at":"2024-07-26T16:44:24Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"32fe5699a91f9fd2412548760ab94ccb6339bfe9","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:arrays","name":"domain:arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4
```","created_at":"2022-06-23T15:08:52Z","updated_at":"2024-07-26T16:44:24Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"32fe5699a91f9fd2412548760ab94ccb6339bfe9","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:arrays","name":"domain:arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, dims = 1, init = 0)`","user":{"login":"N5N3","id":22023842,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIyMDIzODQy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/22023842?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3","html_url":"https://github.com/N5N3","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/N5N3/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This PR make `has_fast_linear_indexing` rely on `IndexStyle`/`ndims` to  fix `mapreduce` for `Broadcasted` with `dim > 1`.
Before:
```julia
julia> a = randn(100,100);

julia> bc = Broadcast.instantiate(Base.broadcasted(+,a,a));

julia> sum(bc,dims = 1,init = 0.0) == sum(collect(bc), dims = 1)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching LinearIndices(::Base.Broadcast.Broadcasted{Base.Broadcast.DefaultArrayStyle{2}, Tuple{Base.OneTo{Int64}, Base.OneTo{Int64}}, typeof(+), Tuple{Matrix{Float64}, Matrix{Float64}}})
```
After:
```julia
julia> sum(bc,dims = 1,init = 0.0) == sum(collect(bc), dims = 1)
true
```

This should extend the optimized fallback to more `AbstractArray`. (e.g. `SubArray`)

Test that the mapreduce infrastructure (mostly) supports broadcasted objects, we can use a lazy Broadcasted on array-likes instead of using an intermediate `map`. This exercises the internals more thoroughly and identifies a few more places where we need to use `::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted` and requires an offset axis bugfix.

Fixes #38558 

<details><summary>local benchmarks</summary>

```julia-repl
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> function f(x, y)
           return @inbounds mapreduce(==,+,x, y)
       end
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> function f2(x, y)
               total=0
               @inbounds for i in 1:length(x)
                       total += x[i]==y[i]
               end
               return total
       end
f2 (generic function with 1 method)

julia> x = randn(10240); y = similar(x);

julia> @btime f2(x,y)
  1.988 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
0

julia> @btime f(x,y)
  2.023 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
0
```
</details>

Alternative to:  #41001 (cc @mcabbott)

Fixes #53417

<details><summary>more benchmarking from 53417</summary>

```julia-repl
julia> x = randn((512,512));

julia> y = randn((512, 512));

julia> g(x) = x^2
g (generic function with 1 method)

julia> @time mapreduce(g,+,x)
  0.031427 seconds (90.73 k allocations: 4.607 MiB, 24.14% gc time, 99.23% compilation time)
262233.95257231314

julia> @time mapreduce(g,+,x)
  0.000064 seconds (1 allocation: 16 bytes)
262233.95257231314

julia> f(x,y) = x * y
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> mapreduce(f, +, x, y);

julia> @time mapreduce(f, +, x, y);
  0.000746 seconds (3 allocations: 80 bytes)
```

</details>","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55301/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55301/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2256698163","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55301#issuecomment-2256698163","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55301","id":2256698163,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Ggnsz","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-29T19:07:44Z","updated_at":"2024-07-29T19:07:44Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> ```julia

As I wrote in the code comment:

> Similar to the scalar mapreduce, there are two*three cases we need to consider
> when constructing the initial results array:
> * Have init: Fill results array with init, proceed (regardless of reduction size)
>   * Don't worry about widening and do a straight iteration with in-place updates over all values
> * Otherwise:
>   * No values: Fill with `mapreduce_empty` & return
>   * One value: Fill with `mapreduce_first` & return
>   * Two+ values: Fill with `op(f(a1), f(a2))`, proceed <--- this is the hard case:
>       * Proceeding involves skipping the first two inner iterations

This is a WIP because I expect it to have some fairly significant performance regressions at the moment. Some may be easy to recover, others may be harder.

Closes: #45566, #47231, #55213, part of the work towards #52397 and #53945, this drastically simplifies the initialization of dimensional reductions and is taking steps towards a unification of the (non-dimensional) `mapreduce` with the `dims` flavor.  As I wrote in the code comment:

> Similar to the scalar mapreduce, there are two*three cases we need to consider
> when constructing the initial results array:
> * Have init: Fill results array with init, proceed (regardless of reduction size)
>   * Don't worry about widening and do a straight iteration with in-place updates over all values
> * Otherwise:
>   * No values: Fill with `mapreduce_empty` & return
>   * One value: Fill with `mapreduce_first` & return
>   * Two+ values: Fill with `op(f(a1), f(a2))`, proceed <--- this is the hard case:
>       * Proceeding involves skipping the first two inner iterations

This is a WIP because I expect it to have some fairly significant performance regressions at the moment. Some may be easy to recover, others may be harder.

part of the work towards #52397 and #53945, this drastically simplifies the initialization of dimensional reductions and is taking steps towards a unification of the (non-dimensional) `mapreduce` with the `dims` flavor.  As I wrote in the code comment:

> Similar to the scalar mapreduce, there are two*three cases we need to consider
> when constructing the initial results array:
> * Have init: Fill results array with init, proceed (regardless of reduction size)
>   * Don't worry about widening and do a straight iteration with in-place updates over all values
> * Otherwise:
>   * No values: Fill with `mapreduce_empty` & return
>   * One value: Fill with `mapreduce_first` & return
>   * Two+ values: Fill with `op(f(a1), f(a2))`, proceed <--- this is the hard case:
>       * Proceeding involves skipping the first two inner iterations

This is a WIP because I expect it to have some fairly significant performance regressions at the moment. Some may be easy to recover, others may be harder.

Closes: #45566, #47231, #55213, #31427, #41054, part of the work towards #52397 and #53945, this drastically simplifies the initialization of dimensional reductions and is taking steps towards a unification of the (non-dimensional) `mapreduce` with the `dims` flavor.  As I wrote in the code comment:

> Similar to the scalar mapreduce, there are two*three cases we need to consider
> when constructing the initial results array:
> * Have init: Fill results array with init, proceed (regardless of reduction size)
>   * Don't worry about widening and do a straight iteration with in-place updates over all values
> * Otherwise:
>   * No values: Fill with `mapreduce_empty` & return
>   * One value: Fill with `mapreduce_first` & return
>   * Two+ values: Fill with `op(f(a1), f(a2))`, proceed <--- this is the hard case:
>       * Proceeding involves skipping the first two inner iterations

This is a WIP because I expect it to have some fairly significant performance regressions at the moment. Some may be easy to recover, others may be harder.

Closes: #45566, closes #47231, closes #55213, closes #31427, closes #41054, closes #54920, closes #42259.

Addresses half of #45562.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55318/reactions","total_count":6,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":6,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55318/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2261530277","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55318#issuecomment-2261530277","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55318","id":2261530277,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6GzDal","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-31T21:58:32Z","updated_at":"2024-07-31T21:58:32Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think I've addressed the most egregious of the performance issues, but I've only done spot tests so far. etc."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-07-29T17:02:21Z","updated_at":"2024-07-31T22:14:00Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":true,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55301","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55301","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55301.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55301.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Now that the mapreduce infrastructure (mostly) supports broadcasted objects, we can use a lazy Broadcasted on array-likes instead of using an intermediate `map`. This exercises the internals more thoroughly and identifies a few more places where we need to use `::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted` and requires an offset axis bugfix.

Fixes #38558 

<details><summary>local benchmarks</summary>

```julia-repl
julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> function f(x, y)
           return @inbounds mapreduce(==,+,x, y)
       end
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> function f2(x, y)
               total=0
               @inbounds for i in 1:length(x)
                       total += x[i]==y[i]
               end
               return total
       end
f2 (generic function with 1 method)

julia> x = randn(10240); y = similar(x);

against `using Foo` in package code (or "using considered harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

 available for direct use. Names can also be used via dot syntax (e.g. `Foo.foo` to access
 the name `foo`), whether they are `export`ed or not.
 See the [manual section about modules](@ref modules) for details.
+
+!!! note
+    Doing `using Foo` in packages or code that you want to keep working with
+    updated dependencies is not recommended. The reason for this is if another
+    dependency starts to export one of the same names as `Foo` the code will
+    error due to an ambiguity in which package the name should be taken from.
+    Instead, explicitly list what names you want to use from Foo, for example:
+    `using Foo: Foo, parsefile, readfile` to get access bring `Foo` and two
+    functions `parsefile` and `readfile` into harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

against `using Foo` in package code (or "using considered harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

against `using Foo` in package code (or "using considered harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

 available for direct use. Names can also be used via dot syntax (e.g. `Foo.foo` to access
 the name `foo`), whether they are `export`ed or not.
 See the [manual section about modules](@ref modules) for details.
+
+!!! note
+    Doing `using Foo` in packages or code that you want to keep working with
+    updated dependencies is not recommended. The reason for this is if another
+    dependency starts to export one of the same names as `Foo` the code will
+    error due to an ambiguity in which package the name should be taken from.
+    Instead, explicitly list what names you want to use from Foo, for example:
+    `using Foo: Foo, parsefile, readfile` to get access bring `Foo` and two
+    functions `parsefile` and `readfile` into scope.","path":"base/docs/basedocs.jl","commit_id":"eb9c6681461b59c2835cdef4d3c7d89adc130ad8","original_commit_id":"eb9c6681461b59c2835cdef4d3c7d89adc130ad8","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Ooooh, I see, #53428 added the annotation to the manual whereas this original text is in the `using` docstring. harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

against `using Foo` in package code (or "using considered harmful")","user":{"login":"KristofferC","id":1282691,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyODI2OTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1282691?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC","html_url":"https://github.com/KristofferC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KristofferC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":106,"created_at":"2021-09-01T17:00:06Z","updated_at":"2024-06-04T14:05:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/42080","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/42080.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I feel we are heading up against a "`using` crisis" where any new feature that is implemented by exporting a new name (either in Base or a package) becomes a breaking change. This is already happening (https://github.com/JuliaGPU/CUDA.jl/pull/1097, https://github.com/JuliaWeb/HTTP.jl/pull/745) and as projects get bigger and more names are exported, the likelihood of this rapidly increases.

The flaw in `using Foo` is fundamental in that you cannot lexically see where a name comes from so when two packages export the same name, you are screwed. Any code that relies on `using Foo` and then using an exported name from `Foo` is vulnerable to another dependency exporting the same name.
Therefore, I think we should start to strongly discourage the use of `using Foo` and only recommend `using Foo` for ephemeral work (e.g. REPL work).

* There is now tooling to help migrate codebases to explicit imports (https://github.com/ericphanson/ExplicitImports.jl)

I think the recommendation (and associated information it contains) is highly valuable, even if you don't agree that this is problematic or worth incorporated into your own personal code style. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824","id":2355779512,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5ynFz6","number":54824,"title":"allow non-identity ranges for `Slice`","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-06-16T13:43:11Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T14:54:50Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54824","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"My understanding is that `Base.Slice(r)` just indicates that the range `r` is a full axis of some array. This is used by `view` to speed up operations (linear indexing). Hence I would expect that `Slice` passes all relevant methods on to its argument range. However, for `axes` and `getindex` this is not the case. These two methods instead assume that the argument range is some identity mapping. Here is an example:
```
julia> r = 2:8
2:8

julia> s = Base.Slice(r)
Base.Slice(2:8)

julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 5)

julia> axes(r)
(Base.OneTo(7),)

julia> axes(s)
(Base.IdentityUnitRange(2:8),)
```
With this PR:
```
julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 6)

julia> axes(r), axes(s)
((Base.OneTo(7),), (Base.OneTo(7),))
```
I've come across this while working on my small package [OffsetRanges.jl](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl). The starting point is the idea that one should get offset arrays easily via `view` once one has offset ranges. I'm trying to see how far this approach can be pushed. As far as I can tell, it actually works very well -- until one uses `Slice` to make operations faster. The branch [OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices) that uses `Slice` does not work with current Julia, but with this PR it does. I find it weird that `view` stops working with offset ranges if one combines them with `Slice`.

I don't know what the original motivation was for assuming identity ranges. Maybe I'm missing something. I've run the `subarray` tests with this PR and they worked fine.

For identity ranges this PR should not introduce any difference. Apart from passing `axes` and `getindex` on to the argument slice, I have removed the methods for `axes1` and `size` because the default method works. I've changed `reindex` to align with `getindex`. I guess that the separate `reindex` method for `Slice` could actually be dropped because in the method for `AbstractVector` the function `maybeview` boils down to `getindex` for `Slice`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2173636703","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824#issuecomment-2173636703","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824","id":2173636703,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6BjxBf","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-17T14:54:49Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T14:54:49Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"`Slice` isn't an exported or public type, and if you manually use it like that you're quite likely to construct a broken `SubArray`.  I suppose we could _also_ add explicit support for checkbounds to ensure it matches the entire axis.

But @jishnub is exactly right here — `Slice`'s mandate has always been to represent `:`s and nothing else.  The broken tests that you've been battling are representative of that assumption.  And while it's not public, folks (like yourself) have used it in packages and are likely assuming the same.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2173636703/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849","id":2360262515,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6Mrr9z","number":54849,"title":"Problem with `similar` called for view-types such as `SubArray`, `ReshapedArray` or `ReinterpretArray`","user":{"login":"RainerHeintzmann","id":7559915,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc1NTk5MTU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7559915?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann","html_url":"https://github.com/RainerHeintzmann","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RainerHeintzmann/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-06-18T16:30:03Z","updated_at":"2024-06-19T14:13:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"There is a problem with the type based `similar` method, applied to a view (`SubArray`), a reshaped array (`Base.ReshapedArray`) or a reinterpreted array (`Base.ReinterpretArray`), since these array modifications doe not have constructors, which can directly be called.
Here is an example (Julia 1.10.4):
```julia
julia> A = @view ones(10,10)[1:20];

julia> similar(typeof(A), axes(A));
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching SubArray{Float64, 1, Vector{Float64}, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}}, true}(::UndefInitializer, ::Tuple{Int64})

Closest candidates are:
  SubArray{T, N, P, I, L}(::Any, ::Any, ::Any, ::Any) where {T, N, P, I, L}
   @ Base subarray.jl:19

Stacktrace:
 [1] similar(::Type{SubArray{Float64, 1, Vector{Float64}, Tuple{UnitRange{Int64}}, true}}, dims::Tuple{Int64})
   @ Base .\abstractarray.jl:877
```
I suggest the following fix in [base/abstractarray.jl](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/a9b48697deff2e53515d3c7611168466ce81f078/base/abstractarray.jl#L845):
```julia
Base.similar(::Type{TA}, dims::Dims) where {T,N,P,TA<:SubArray{T,N,P}} = similar(P, dims)
Base.similar(::Type{TA}, dims::Dims) where {T,N,P,TA<:Base.ReshapedArray{T,N,P}} = similar(P, dims)
Base.similar(::Type{TA}, dims::Dims) where {T,N,O,P,TA<:Base.ReinterpretArray{T,N,O,P}} = similar(P, dims)
```
This should then unravel nested version of these array views.
The exact Version details:
```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.4
Commit 48d4fd4843 (2024-06-04 10:41 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Windows (x86_64-w64-mingw32)
  CPU: 16 × 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-11800H @ 2.30GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, tigerlake)
Threads: 16 default, 0 interactive, 8 GC (on 16 virtual cores)
Environment:
  JULIA_NUM_THREADS = auto
  JULIA_EDITOR = code
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2178825331","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849#issuecomment-2178825331","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54849","id":2178825331,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6B3jxz","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-19T14:13:29Z","updated_at":"2024-06-19T14:13:29Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"This seems like a good idea!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2178825331/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
