array","user":{"login":"MilesCranmer","id":7593028,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjc1OTMwMjg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7593028?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer","html_url":"https://github.com/MilesCranmer","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/MilesCranmer/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:arrays","name":"domain:arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":6,"created_at":"2024-06-26T00:28:35Z","updated_at":"2024-06-27T15:52:52Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Because of the fact that broadcasted zero-dimensional arrays automatically convert to scalars (https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30825) –

```julia
julia> x = zeros(Float64)
0-dimensional Array{Float64, 0}:
0.0

julia> x .+ x
0.0

julia> x + x
0-dimensional Array{Float64, 0}:
0.0
```

it would be nice to have `Array{T}` as a constructor for zero-dimensional arrays. For example, if I write out:

```julia
f(x::Array{T}) where {T} = Array{T}(x .+ x)
```

This works for most cases:

```julia
julia> f(ones(Float64, 1))
1-element Vector{Float64}:
 2.0

julia> f(ones(Float64, 1, 1))
1×1 Matrix{Float64}:
 2.0

julia> f(ones(Float64, 1, 1, 1))
1×1×1 Array{Float64, 3}:
[:, :, 1] =
 2.0
```

but fails for zero-dimensional arrays:

```julia
julia> f(ones(Float64))
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching (Array{Float64})(::Float64)
```

I think that `Array{T}(::T)` returning `fill(T)` seems to be a reasonable choice.

Right now if you try to write a generic array converter, you either have something like:

```julia
julia> x = zeros(Float64)
0-dimensional Array{Float64, 0}:
0.0

julia> Float32.(x)
0.0f0
```

meaning zero-dimensional arrays get turned into scalars. So one option is to use something like

```julia
AbstractArray{Float32}(x)
```
for a converter, which works for 0-dimensional arrays:

```julia
julia> AbstractArray{Float32}(x)
0-dimensional Array{Float32, 0}:
0.0
```

but instead, fails on scalars, such as the result of `x .+ x`

```julia
julia> AbstractArray{Float32}(x .+ x)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching (AbstractArray{Float32})(::Float64)
```

which means it's a bit of a sharp edge to write generic operations for any `AbstractArray{T,N}` unless imposing the restriction `N>0`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54935/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54935/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2195081354","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54935#issuecomment-2195081354","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54935","id":2195081354,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6C1kiK","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-27T15:52:51Z","updated_at":"2024-06-27T15:52:51Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Isn't this effectively a duplicate (or, rather a downstream effect) of #28866?  It seems like making `Array{Any}(x)` sometimes return a `reshape(Any[x])` and sometimes return `Any[elt for elt in x]` would just promulgate exactly the sorts of behaviors you're tripping over here even farther, wouldn't it?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2195081354/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

call"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":471,"created_at":"2017-12-08T19:10:10Z","updated_at":"2024-05-02T17:15:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/24990","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR addresses #554, #5571, and #22710 by "currying" underscores in function calls like `f(_,y)` into anonymous function expressions `x -> f(x,y)`.  (Note that `_.foo` works and turns into `x -> x.foo` since it is equivalent to a `getfield` call, and `_[i]` works and turns into `x -> x[i]`, since it is equivalent to a `getindex(_,i)` call.)

This will help us get rid of functions like `equalto` (#23812) or `occursin` (#24967), is useful for "destructuring" as discussed in #22710, and should generally be convenient in lots of cases to avoid having to explicitly do `x -> f(x,y)`.

Some simplifying design decisions that I made:

* The currying is "tight", i.e. it only converts the immediately surrounding function call into a lambda, [as suggested by](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/22710#issuecomment-314259250) @JeffBezanson (and [as in Scala](https://www.scala-lang.org/files/archive/spec/2.11/06-expressions.html#placeholder-syntax-for-anonymous-functions)).   So, e.g. `f(g(_,y))` is equivalent to `f(x -> g(x,y))`.   (Note that something like `find(!(_ in c), y)` will work fine, because the `!` operator works on functions; you can also use `_ ∉ c`.)  Any other rule seems hard to make comprehensible and consistent.

* ~~Only a single underscore is allowed.  `f(_,_)` throws an error: this case seems ambiguous to me (do you want `x -> f(x,x)` or `x,y -> f(x,y)`?), so it seemed better to punt on this for now.  We can always add a meaning for multiple underscores later.~~ Similar to Scala, multiple underscores are converted into multiple arguments in the order they appear.  e.g. `f(_,y,_)` is equivalent to `(x,z) -> f(x,y,z)`.  See [rationale below](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-350879561).

The implementation is pretty trivial.  If people are in favor, I will add
- [x] Tests
- [x] Documentation
- [ ] Fix interaction with broadcasting `f.(x, _)`
- [ ] Fix interaction with keyword arguments `f(x; y=_)`
- [ ] Support chained comparisons, e.g. `3 ≤ _ ≤ 10`, since they parse as a single expression?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/reactions","total_count":300,"+1":178,"-1":4,"laugh":0,"hooray":13,"confused":2,"heart":87,"rocket":10,"eyes":6},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2091100323","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/24990#issuecomment-2091100323","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/24990","id":2091100323,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM58o6ij","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-02T17:15:35Z","updated_at":"2024-05-02T17:15:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Then the secondary sin is that Julia's syntax is fancy enough that it can be tricky to know what "counts" as a single function call.  Is tuple construction `(1, 2, _)` a single function call?  What about `Expr(:curly)`s?  Or `_()`?  Or `_[]`?  Or `0 <= _ < 1`?  Or `x + _ + y`?  Or `x +̂ _ +̂ y`?  Or `_'`?  Or `[_]`? Or `x ? _ : y`?  Or `_::T`?  Obviously, you can draw lines and reasons for each and every one of these things to act one way or the other, but my point is that those lines are squiggly and weave their way from one syntax to the next.

IMO, we need a clear precedence boundary.  `->` gives us that in a way that everyone already understands.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2091100323/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Macros are another.  Giving up on it altogether and just using `x->x` is also ok in my book.

It's possible there exists a simple rule without a "fence", but goodness the edge cases are tricky.

For example, the current implementation here notes that we'd need to add special support for broadcasting and kwcalls and chained comparisons — those are are broken because they are internally composed of multiple expressions.  They also all feel like they have an obvious answer.  But there's so many more cases.  With the current implementation here:
* `(;_...)` is `x->(;x...)` but `(_...,)` is an error
* `_[]` is `x->x[]` and `_{1}` is `x->x{1}` but `_()` is an error
* `_[end]` is `x->x[y->lastindex(y)]` (an error)
* `A[_] = 1` creates a closure that does the `setindex!`, but then throws it away and you are only left with the value `1` (the RHS); this should probably be an error
* `A[_] .= 1` is similar, but errors as the closure ends up in internal machinery
* `_ & a` is `x->x & a` but `_ && a` is an error (`_` as rvalue)
* `b ? _ : nothing` feels like it should work but it's really no different from `if b; _; else; nothing; end` which seems more obvious it shouldn't
* `"$_"` is `x->"$x"` and `"$_ $_"("hello","world")` even works but `` `$_` `` is an error (puts the wrong closure through some internal machinery)
* `(i for i in _)` is `x->(i for i in x)` (actually works!) but `[i for i in _]` is an error
* `a += _` defines a closure that tries to add itself to its argument and then names it `a`.

What's the rule? Or is it just a whole pile of special cases?

My dreams of a fenceless `_` syntax are well and truly dead.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2093613274/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

number.","creator":{"login":"StefanKarpinski","id":153596,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1MzU5Ng==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/153596?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski","html_url":"https://github.com/StefanKarpinski","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/StefanKarpinski/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"open_issues":100,"closed_issues":32,"state":"open","created_at":"2016-08-18T13:36:22Z","updated_at":"2024-05-13T17:53:20Z","due_on":null,"closed_at":null},"comments":16,"created_at":"2024-05-12T17:00:11Z","updated_at":"2024-05-16T19:05:56Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54447","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54447","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54447.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54447.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"I noticed that

```julia
julia> '４' |> isdigit
false
```

even though

```julia
julia> '４'
'４': Unicode U+FF14 (category Nd: Number, decimal digit)
```

The docstring doesn't say that this only checks ASCII, and the unicode category (see [here](https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr44/#Numeric_Type)) is an exact match for the definition (ten glyphs making up the numbers zero through nine, in various scripts). So while some things will still slip through (e.g. [`四`](https://jisho.org/word/%E5%9B%9B), which is considered a letter by Unicode), this implementation is a tiny bit more correct.

However, it's not just roses and sunshine; there is a downside, in that this won't vectorize anymore due to the `ccall` behind `category_code`. We could bring the double table lookup into pure Julia, but I'm not sure whether that would restore performance here. Maybe it could? As is, this PR comes with a 100x regression in a microbenchmark.

v1.11-alpha2:

```julia
julia> @benchmark count(isdigit, v) setup=(v=rand(Char, 10_000))
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 202 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  392.871 ns … 509.455 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     400.743 ns               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   405.332 ns ±  12.212 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

       ▂▅█▆▇▆▄▂                  ▁▁   ▁▃▂▂▂                     ▂
  ▆▂▅▅██████████▇▆▅▄▄▄▄▂▄▄▄▅▅▅▆▆▇██▇▇▆███████▇▇▇▆▇▆▆▇▆▆▆▇█████▇ █
  393 ns        Histogram: log(frequency) by time        446 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

This PR:

```julia
julia> @benchmark count(isdigit, v) setup=(v=rand(Char, 10_000))
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1 evaluation.
 Range (min … max):  40.870 μs … 483.089 μs  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     42.200 μs               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   45.848 μs ±  19.561 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

  █▆▃▃▂                                                        ▁
  ████████▆▆▄▄▆▄▅▅▅▄▅▅▇▄▄▄▄▄▁▁▁▃▁▃▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▃▇██▇▅▆▃▆█▄▄▄▃▁▁▁▁▁█ █
  40.9 μs       Histogram: log(frequency) by time       129 μs <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

In a single-invocation benchmark, we "merely" have a 2-3x/10ns regression:

v1.11-alpha2:

```julia
julia> @benchmark isdigit(v) setup=(v=rand(Char)) evals=1
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1 evaluation.
 Range (min … max):  10.000 ns … 40.000 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     20.000 ns              ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   17.548 ns ±  4.491 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

                                                            █  
  ▇▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁█ ▂
  10 ns           Histogram: frequency by time          20 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

This PR:

```julia
julia> @benchmark isdigit(v) setup=(v=rand(Char)) evals=1
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1 evaluation.
 Range (min … max):  20.000 ns … 520.000 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     30.000 ns               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   32.170 ns ±  31.893 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

  ▇  █  ▂                      ▂   ▂                           ▁
  █▁▁█▁▁█▁▁▇▁▁▁▅▁▁▄▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▆▁▁█▁▁▁█▁▁█▁▁▅▁▁▁▄▁▁▃▁▁▄▁▁▁▃▁▁▇▁▁▅ █
  20 ns         Histogram: log(frequency) by time       200 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

That being said, we already check `category_code` pretty much everywhere else, and this microbenchmark is extremely unlikely to be representative of an actual workload. {"action":"closed","number":54447,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54447","id":1865369148,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5vL0Y8","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54447","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54447.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54447.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54447","number":54447,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Ensure non-ASCII decimal digits are also `isdigit`","user":{"login":"Seelengrab","id":11753998,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzUzOTk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11753998?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab","html_url":"https://github.com/Seelengrab","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I noticed that

```julia
julia> '４' |> isdigit
false
```

even though

```julia
julia> '４'
'４': Unicode U+FF14 (category Nd: Number, decimal digit)
```

The docstring doesn't say that this only checks ASCII, and the unicode category (see [here](https://www.unicode.org/reports/tr44/#Numeric_Type)) is an exact match for the definition (ten glyphs making up the numbers zero through nine, in various scripts). So while some things will still slip through (e.g. [`四`](https://jisho.org/word/%E5%9B%9B), which is considered a letter by Unicode), this implementation is a tiny bit more correct.

However, it's not just roses and sunshine; there is a downside, in that this won't vectorize anymore due to the `ccall` behind `category_code`. We could bring the double table lookup into pure Julia, but I'm not sure whether that would restore performance here. Maybe it could? As is, this PR comes with a 100x regression in a microbenchmark.

v1.11-alpha2:

```julia
julia> @benchmark count(isdigit, v) setup=(v=rand(Char, 10_000))
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 202 evaluations.
 Range (min … max):  392.871 ns … 509.455 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     400.743 ns               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   405.332 ns ±  12.212 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

       ▂▅█▆▇▆▄▂                  ▁▁   ▁▃▂▂▂                     ▂
  ▆▂▅▅██████████▇▆▅▄▄▄▄▂▄▄▄▅▅▅▆▆▇██▇▇▆███████▇▇▇▆▇▆▆▇▆▆▆▇█████▇ █
  393 ns        Histogram: log(frequency) by time        446 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

This PR:

```julia
julia> @benchmark count(isdigit, v) setup=(v=rand(Char, 10_000))
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1 evaluation.
 Range (min … max):  40.870 μs … 483.089 μs  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     42.200 μs               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   45.848 μs ±  19.561 μs  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

  █▆▃▃▂                                                        ▁
  ████████▆▆▄▄▆▄▅▅▅▄▅▅▇▄▄▄▄▄▁▁▁▃▁▃▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▃▇██▇▅▆▃▆█▄▄▄▃▁▁▁▁▁█ █
  40.9 μs       Histogram: log(frequency) by time       129 μs <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

In a single-invocation benchmark, we "merely" have a 2-3x/10ns regression:

v1.11-alpha2:

```julia
julia> @benchmark isdigit(v) setup=(v=rand(Char)) evals=1
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1 evaluation.
 Range (min … max):  10.000 ns … 40.000 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     20.000 ns              ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   17.548 ns ±  4.491 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

                                                            █  
  ▇▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁█ ▂
  10 ns           Histogram: frequency by time          20 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

This PR:

```julia
julia> @benchmark isdigit(v) setup=(v=rand(Char)) evals=1
BenchmarkTools.Trial: 10000 samples with 1 evaluation.
 Range (min … max):  20.000 ns … 520.000 ns  ┊ GC (min … max): 0.00% … 0.00%
 Time  (median):     30.000 ns               ┊ GC (median):    0.00%
 Time  (mean ± σ):   32.170 ns ±  31.893 ns  ┊ GC (mean ± σ):  0.00% ± 0.00%

  ▇  █  ▂                      ▂   ▂                           ▁
  █▁▁█▁▁█▁▁▇▁▁▁▅▁▁▄▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▁▆▁▁█▁▁▁█▁▁█▁▁▅▁▁▁▄▁▁▃▁▁▄▁▁▁▃▁▁▇▁▁▅ █
  20 ns         Histogram: log(frequency) by time       200 ns <

 Memory estimate: 0 bytes, allocs estimate: 0.
```

That being said, we already check `category_code` pretty much everywhere else, and this microbenchmark is extremely unlikely to be representative of an actual workload. I installed Julia with the command `curl -fsSL https://install.julialang.org | sh`. I couldn't find anyone else talking about this specific issue anywhere I looked, but I apologize if I just missed it. There seems to be a problem displaying `reinterpret`ed/`reshape`ed 0-dimensional arrays. Despite the fact that the elements of this array are `Int64`s, they are shown as 0-dimensional arrays of `Int64`s with unrelated contents. The setup is shown below.
```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.3
Commit 0b4590a5507 (2024-04-30 10:59 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin22.4.0)
  CPU: 10 × Apple M1 Max
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, apple-m1)
Threads: 10 default, 0 interactive, 5 GC (on 8 virtual cores)
Environment:
  JULIA_NUM_THREADS = 10

julia> r = reinterpret(reshape, Int, fill(1 => 2))
2-element reinterpret(reshape, Int64, ::Array{Pair{Int64, Int64}, 0}) with eltype Int64:
 fill(4538293232)
 fill(4539746992)

julia> r[1], r[2], collect(r)
(1, 2, [1, 2])

julia> typeof(r)
Base.ReinterpretArray{Int64, 1, Pair{Int64, Int64}, Array{Pair{Int64, Int64}, 0}, true}

julia> typeof(fill(4538293232))
Array{Int64, 0}

julia> eltype(r)
Int64
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54623/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54623/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2138166898","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54623#issuecomment-2138166898","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54623","id":2138166898,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5_cdZy","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-29T19:59:42Z","updated_at":"2024-05-29T19:59:42Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The issue here is with the ReshapeArray's index style and accessing with more than one index:

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1620960567","pull_request_review_id":2088633202,"id":1620960567,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5gneU3","diff_hunk":"@@ -93,6 +93,82 @@ function _dropdims(A::AbstractArray, dims::Dims)
 end
 _dropdims(A::AbstractArray, dim::Integer) = _dropdims(A, (Int(dim),))
 
+
+"""
+    insertdims(A; dims)
+
+Return an array with the same data as `A`, but with singleton dimensions specified by
+`dims` inserted.
+The dimensions of `A` and `dims` must be contiguous.
+If dimensions occur multiple times in `dims`, several singleton dimensions are inserted.
+
+The result shares the same underlying data as `A`, such that the
+result is mutable if and only if `A` is mutable, and setting elements of one
+alters the values of the other.
+
+See also: [`reshape`](@ref), [`dropdims`](@ref), [`vec`](@ref).
+
+# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
+2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
+ 1  2
+ 3  4
+
+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
+1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =
+ 1
+
+[:, :, 2, 1] =
+ 3
+
+[:, :, 1, 2] =

+
+[:, :, 2, 2] =
+ 4

+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
+1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =

+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))

+2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
+ 5  2
+ 3  4

+"""
+insertdims(A; dims) = _insertdims(A, dims)
+function _insertdims(A::AbstractArray{T, N}, dims::Tuple{Vararg{Int64, M}}) where {T, N, M}
+    maximum(dims) ≤ ndims(A)+1 || throw(ArgumentError("The largest entry in dims must be ≤ ndims(A) + 1."))
+    1 ≤ minimum(dims) || throw(ArgumentError("The smallest entry in dims must be ≥ 1."))
+    issorted(dims) || throw(ArgumentError("dims=$(dims) are not sorted"))","path":"base/abstractarraymath.jl","commit_id":"50ae007d83a489aeee04704132badf834c25f1f5","original_commit_id":"50ae007d83a489aeee04704132badf834c25f1f5","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This will allow repeated dimensions like `insertdims(A, dims=(1,1,1))`... but I don't think the implementation (either your old one or my proposed one) works with repeated dims. My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix

 2  4

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1620971568","pull_request_review_id":2088658783,"id":1620971568,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5gnhAw","diff_hunk":"@@ -93,6 +93,82 @@ function _dropdims(A::AbstractArray, dims::Dims)
 end
 _dropdims(A::AbstractArray, dim::Integer) = _dropdims(A, (Int(dim),))
 
+
+"""
+    insertdims(A; dims)
+
+Return an array with the same data as `A`, but with singleton dimensions specified by
+`dims` inserted.
+The dimensions of `A` and `dims` must be contiguous.
+If dimensions occur multiple times in `dims`, several singleton dimensions are inserted.
+
+The result shares the same underlying data as `A`, such that the
+result is mutable if and only if `A` is mutable, and setting elements of one
+alters the values of the other.
+
+See also: [`reshape`](@ref), [`dropdims`](@ref), [`vec`](@ref).
+
+# Examples
+```jldoctest
+julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
+2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
+ 1  2
+ 3  4
+
+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
+1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =
+ 1
+
+[:, :, 2, 1] =
+ 3
+
+[:, :, 1, 2] =
+ 2
+
+[:, :, 2, 2] =
+ 4
+
+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
+1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =
+ 1  3
+
+[:, :, 1, 2] =
+ 2  4
+
+julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
+1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
+[:, :, 1, 1] =
+ 1  3
+
+[:, :, 2, 1] =
+ 2  4
+
+julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
+2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
+ 5  2
+ 3  4
+```
+"""
+insertdims(A; dims) = _insertdims(A, dims)
+function _insertdims(A::AbstractArray{T, N}, dims::Tuple{Vararg{Int64, M}}) where {T, N, M}
+    maximum(dims) ≤ ndims(A)+1 || throw(ArgumentError("The largest entry in dims must be ≤ ndims(A) + 1."))
+    1 ≤ minimum(dims) || throw(ArgumentError("The smallest entry in dims must be ≥ 1."))
+    issorted(dims) || throw(ArgumentError("dims=$(dims) are not sorted"))","path":"base/abstractarraymath.jl","commit_id":"50ae007d83a489aeee04704132badf834c25f1f5","original_commit_id":"50ae007d83a489aeee04704132badf834c25f1f5","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Oh I see — that's great. all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4

all!

My first attempt to add a method which we think is quite helpful.
It is similar to `dropdims` but basically the inverse to it. I tried to follow the implementation of `dropdims`. It also looks good in `@code_warntype`.

I would be happy to receive some feedback and suggestions :)

Best,

Felix


Example:
```julia
julia> a = [1 2; 3 4]
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 1  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,3))
1×2×2×1 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 1  3

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 2  4

julia> b[1,1,1,1] = 5; a
2×2 Matrix{Int64}:
 5  2
 3  4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,1))
1×1×2×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5

[:, :, 2, 1] =
 3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2

[:, :, 2, 2] =
 4

julia> b = insertdims(a, dims=(1,2))
1×2×1×2 Array{Int64, 4}:
[:, :, 1, 1] =
 5  3

[:, :, 1, 2] =
 2  4
```","created_at":"2022-06-23T15:08:52Z","updated_at":"2024-05-30T15:36:35Z","closed_at":null,"merged_at":null,"merge_commit_sha":"c900addb5621a0f57658a1428b8cb070a6d3bc4b","assignee":null,"assignees":[],"requested_reviewers":[],"requested_teams":[],"labels":[{"id":62693664,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MjY5MzY2NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/domain:arrays","name":"domain:arrays","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":"[a, AbstractRange"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-03-23T13:28:03Z","updated_at":"2024-04-02T14:45:10Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53827","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53827","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53827.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53827.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Currently, users wishing to customize `x::Number .+ r::MyAbstractUnitRange` need to extend not only
```julia
Broadcast.broadcasted(::Broadcast.DefaultArrayStyle{1}, ::typeof(+), x::Number, r::MyAbstractUnitRange)
```
but also other methods such as
```julia
Broadcast.broadcasted(::Broadcast.DefaultArrayStyle{1}, ::typeof(+), x::Integer, r::MyAbstractUnitRange)
Broadcast.broadcasted(::Broadcast.DefaultArrayStyle{1}, ::typeof(+), x::Real, r::MyAbstractUnitRange)
```
to avoid method ambiguities. This list is also somewhat arbitrary, depending on which methods have been defined for a particular `AbstractRange` type.

This PR makes this optional by forwarding the operation to an internal function, and one would only need to extend the general method for it to work as expected. macros `@which`, `@edit`, `@functionloc`, `@less` from `InteractiveUtils`, if 
applied to the case of literal powers, like `a^12` or `2^-1` used to direct the
user to function `^`, while the compiler generates code for `Base.literal_pow`.

Now the user is shown the code, the compiler generates.

Fixes #53691
Fixes #43337
Fixes #21014

Observation: the `literal_pow` code is generated only if `x` is a signed integer constant with `-2^61 <= x < 2^61`.

case.","user":{"login":"KlausC","id":6223854,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMjM4NTQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6223854?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC","html_url":"https://github.com/KlausC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The macros `@which`, `@edit`, `@functionloc`, `@less` from `InteractiveUtils`, if 
applied to the case of literal powers, like `a^12` or `2^-1` used to direct the
user to function `^`, while the compiler generates code for `Base.literal_pow`.

Now the user is shown the code, the compiler generates.

Fixes #53691
Fixes #43337
Fixes #21014

Observation: the `literal_pow` code is generated only if `x` is a signed integer constant with `-2^61 <= x < 2^61`.

{"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43520","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43520/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43520/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43520/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/43520","id":1086539785,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Aw0gJ","number":43520,"title":"Update libunwind to 1.6","user":{"login":"mkitti","id":8062771,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjgwNjI3NzE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8062771?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti","html_url":"https://github.com/mkitti","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mkitti/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2021-12-22T07:37:55Z","updated_at":"2024-04-03T20:46:45Z","closed_at":"2024-04-03T20:46:45Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This is an issue collecting information about a ABI break in libunwind 1.6.x.

https://abi-laboratory.pro/index.php?view=compat_report&l=libunwind&v1=1.5.0&v2=1.6.0&obj=8ee57&kind=abi

A field `load_offset` was added to `unw_dyn_info_t` in https://github.com/libunwind/libunwind/commit/a4014f33775321b4106a1134b89020a7774902dd breaking the ABI.

{"action":"closed","number":53713,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53713","id":1768339975,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5pZroH","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53713","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53713.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53713.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53713","number":53713,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"fix macros `@which`, `@edit`, `@functionloc`, `@less` for `literal_pow` case.","user":{"login":"KlausC","id":6223854,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYyMjM4NTQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6223854?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC","html_url":"https://github.com/KlausC","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/KlausC/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The macros `@which`, `@edit`, `@functionloc`, `@less` from `InteractiveUtils`, if 
applied to the case of literal powers, like `a^12` or `2^-1` used to direct the
user to function `^`, while the compiler generates code for `Base.literal_pow`.

Now the user is shown the code, the compiler generates.

Fixes #53691
Fixes #43337
Fixes #21014

Observation: the `literal_pow` code is generated only if `x` is a signed integer constant with `-2^61 <= x < 2^61`.

{"repository_id":1644196,"push_id":17836359088,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/master","head":"286e3393fe5102f9f4f1578b3bae863e72f85cbd","before":"12c93910344d34d65222f7c52932b592d74299f2","commits":[{"sha":"286e3393fe5102f9f4f1578b3bae863e72f85cbd","author":{"email":"7e179f4ea6dca441580f3c1b8a3ab438f62a0ec8@users.noreply.github.com","name":"Klaus Crusius"},"message":"fix macros `@which`, `@edit`, `@functionloc`, `@less` for `literal_pow` case. (#53713)

The macros `@which`, `@edit`, `@functionloc`, `@less` from `InteractiveUtils`, if
applied to the case of literal powers, like `a^12` or `2^-1` used to direct the
user to function `^`, while the compiler generates code for `Base.literal_pow`.

Now the user is shown the code the compiler generates.

Fixes #53691
Fixes #43337
Fixes #21014

The goal here is ~~threefold~~ fivefold:

* Document that `init` provides the return value if the collections are empty (status quo)
* Document how reductions over empty collections may be errors (status quo)
* Remove the restriction that `init` must be a "neutral element" (**change**)
* Guarantee that `init` is used exactly once (**change** from being completely unspecified)
* Guarantee that `init` is used as the left-most argument in the reduction (**change** from being unspecified)

And in the process, this cleans up the language around associativity a bit.

This is a draft as this language needs to be propagated through to all the "special" reduction functions like `sum` et al.  But it gives us something concrete to {"action":"opened","number":53945,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/53945","id":1805684650,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5roI-q","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53945.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53945","number":53945,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"RFC: require reduce-like functions to use `init` one or more times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` provides the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in a reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

a shot at revamped documentation in light of https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49042.  The goal here is ~~threefold~~ fivefold:

* Document that `init` provides the return value if the collections are empty (status quo)
* Document how reductions over empty collections may be errors (status quo)
* Remove the restriction that `init` must be a "neutral element" (**change**)
* Guarantee that `init` is used exactly once (**change** from being completely unspecified)
* Guarantee that `init` is used as the left-most argument in the reduction (**change** from being unspecified)

And in the process, this cleans up the language around associativity a bit.

This is a draft as this language needs to be propagated through to all the "special" reduction functions like `sum` et al.  But it gives us something concrete to consider.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53871/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53871/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2035692035","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53871#issuecomment-2035692035","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53871","id":2035692035,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM55VjID","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-03T22:12:35Z","updated_at":"2024-04-03T22:12:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"OK, I would still like to improve our semantics here. here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` provides the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in a reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes times","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"OK, here's take two, building on the work that was done in #53871.  We preserve the neutral element requirement but guarantee that `init` is used to start all evaluation chains.

The news summarizes as succinctly as I can:

> `init` is now guaranteed to be used for non-empty collections one or more times. This ensures that all calls
  to the reducing function `op` take one argument from `itr` and the other from either `init` or the result from a
  previous `op` evaluation. Previously it was explicitly allowed to be omitted from the reduction entirely.

And the docs for `mapreduce` say a bit more:

> If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` take one argument from the (mapped) collection(s) and the other
from either `init` or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that 
does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.

Closes arrays"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":{"login":"rayegun","id":9300055,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjkzMDAwNTU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/9300055?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun","html_url":"https://github.com/rayegun","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"assignees":[{"login":"rayegun","id":9300055,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjkzMDAwNTU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/9300055?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun","html_url":"https://github.com/rayegun","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rayegun/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-04-02T15:27:56Z","updated_at":"2024-04-04T15:12:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If you try to use `filter!` on a `SparseVector`, it fails with an internal method error for `deleteat!`. And then your sparse vector is corrupted:

```julia
julia> using SparseArrays

julia> x = sparsevec(1:2:7, 1:4, 8)
8-element SparseVector{Int64, Int64} with 4 stored entries:
  [1]  =  1
  [3]  =  2
  [5]  =  3
  [7]  =  4

julia> filter!(iseven, x)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching deleteat!(::SparseVector{Int64, Int64}, ::UnitRange{Int64})

Closest candidates are:
  deleteat!(::Vector, ::AbstractUnitRange{<:Integer})
   @ Base array.jl:1525
  deleteat!(::Vector, ::AbstractVector)
   @ Base array.jl:1567
  deleteat!(::Vector, ::Any)
   @ Base array.jl:1566
  ...

Stacktrace:
 [1] filter!(f::typeof(iseven), a::SparseVector{Int64, Int64})
   @ Base .\array.jl:2662
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[23]:1

julia> x
8-element SparseVector{Int64, Int64} with 6 stored entries:
  [1]  =  0
  [2]  =  2
  [3]  =  0
  [4]  =  0
  [5]  =  4
  [7]  =  4
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53920/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53920/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2037482664","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53920#issuecomment-2037482664","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53920","id":2037482664,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM55cYSo","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-04T15:12:17Z","updated_at":"2024-04-04T15:12:17Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I agree this is a bug — it's not just sparse, this method will corrupt _all_ vectors that are mutable but not mutably-sized.

There are three classes of functions:
* Primitives and some other methods are only defined on concrete types: `deleteat!`, `insert!`, `push!`, `pushfirst`, `pop!`, `popat!`, `popfirst!`, `empty!`, `splice!`
* Some abstract methods are implemented for `AbstractVector` but would immediately fail (because they effectively `resize!` first): `append!`, `prepend!`
* But then there are some abstract methods that will fail halfway through: `filter!`, `intersect!`, `union!`, `symdiff!`

I may have missed a few, but this isn't an abstract "yeah, anything can fail in the middle" sort of thing.  We **know** that many `AbstractVectors` are mutable but not mutable-length.  I think all these methods should check for applicability of what they expect to use to `resize!` before doing any preparatory work.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2037482664/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53958","id":2226446500,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6EtOCk","number":53958,"title":"Performance of function totally changes if it first errors?","user":{"login":"Tortar","id":68152031,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY4MTUyMDMx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/68152031?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar","html_url":"https://github.com/Tortar","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Tortar/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-04-04T20:27:07Z","updated_at":"2024-04-04T20:38:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I was reading the thread at https://discourse.julialang.org/t/julia-slower-than-python-for-finding-pangrams/112481 and I found a very strange behaviour, consider this code to be run on the REPL:

```julia
input = ""

function ispangram10(input::String)
    a,A,z,Z = UInt8('a'),UInt8('A'),UInt8('z'),UInt8('Z')
    appears::UInt32 = ~((one(UInt32)<<26)-one(UInt32))
    @inbounds for b in codeunits(input)
        if A ≤ b ≤ Z
            appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-A)
        elseif a ≤ b ≤ z
            appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-a)
        end
        appears == (-1%UInt32) && return true
    end
    return false
end
           
ispangram10(input) # this will error

shift_safe(::Type{T}, s::Integer) where {T} = s & (8 * sizeof(T) - 1)

ispangram10(input)
@time ispangram10(input)
```

you will see that the last timing is

```julia
julia> @time ispangram10(input)
  0.000032 seconds (137 allocations: 2.141 KiB)
```

now if you instead run this:

```julia
input = ""

function ispangram10(input::String)
    a,A,z,Z = UInt8('a'),UInt8('A'),UInt8('z'),UInt8('Z')
    appears::UInt32 = ~((one(UInt32)<<26)-one(UInt32))
    @inbounds for b in codeunits(input)
        if A ≤ b ≤ Z
            appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-A)
        elseif a ≤ b ≤ z
            appears |= one(UInt32) << shift_safe(UInt32, b-a)
        end
        appears == (-1%UInt32) && return true
    end
    return false
end
           
#ispangram10(input) # this is the only difference, it is commented in this version

shift_safe(::Type{T}, s::Integer) where {T} = s & (8 * sizeof(T) - 1)

ispangram10(input)
@time ispangram10(input)
```

it returns instead

```julia
julia> @time ispangram10(input)
  0.000002 seconds
```

What is it happening here? Why the error in the first version makes the function perf much worse afterwards?

```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.2
Commit bd47eca2c8a (2024-03-01 10:14 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 12 × AMD Ryzen 5 5600H with Radeon Graphics
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, znver3)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 12 virtual cores)
```

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/comments/1553807355","pull_request_review_id":1983433840,"id":1553807355,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABkWpM5cnTf7","diff_hunk":"@@ -280,29 +280,65 @@ mapreduce_impl(f, op, A::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted, ifirst::Integer, ilast::Int
 """
     mapreduce(f, op, itrs...; [init])
 
-Apply function `f` to each element(s) in `itrs`, and then reduce the result using the binary
-function `op`. If provided, `init` must be a neutral element for `op` that will be returned
-for empty collections. It is unspecified whether `init` is used for non-empty collections.
-In general, it will be necessary to provide `init` to work with empty collections.
+Apply function `f` to each element(s) in `itrs`, and then repeatedly call the 2 argument
+function `op` with those results or results from previous `op` evaluations until a single value is returned.
+
+If provided, `init` is the return value for empty collections and is used one or more times as
+an argument to `op` for non-empty collections. The `init` value is not transformed by the function `f`.
+Using `init` ensures that all calls to `op` have one argument that is either `init`
+or the result from a previous `op` evaluation, and the ordering of these arguments is
+unspecified. As it may appear in the reduction one or more times, it must be a neutral element for `op` that
+does not change the result by being used more than once. It is generally an error to call `mapreduce`
+with empty collections without specifying an `init` value, but in unambiguous cases an
+identity value for `op` may be returned; see [`Base.reduce_empty`](@ref) for more details.
+
+In contrast with [`mapfoldl`](@ref) and [`mapfoldr`](@ref), the sequence of
+function evaluations and the associativity of the reduction is not specified
+and may vary between different methods and across Julia versions. Some implementations
+may reuse the return value of `f` for elements that appear multiple times in the
+collection(s).","path":"base/reduce.jl","commit_id":"05c9e7a668446b95430d212bcc1e1abeef56a01c","original_commit_id":"05c9e7a668446b95430d212bcc1e1abeef56a01c","user":{"login":"mbauman","id":154641,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1NDY0MQ==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/154641?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman","html_url":"https://github.com/mbauman","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mbauman/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is not a new part of the docstring, though.
